examples.title: Algorithm: RAID Level Simulations
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how different RAID levels handle data distribution, parity calculation, and fault tolerance
examples.code:
ALGORITHM RAID_Level_Selection
INPUT: number_of_disks, desired_raid_level, disk_capacity_gb
OUTPUT: total_usable_capacity, fault_tolerance, description

BEGIN
 SWITCH desired_raid_level
 CASE 0:
 RETURN RAID_0(disk_capacity_gb, number_of_disks)
 CASE 1:
 RETURN RAID_1(disk_capacity_gb, number_of_disks)
 CASE 5:
 RETURN RAID_5(disk_capacity_gb, number_of_disks)
 CASE 6:
 RETURN RAID_6(disk_capacity_gb, number_of_disks)
 CASE 10:
 RETURN RAID_10(disk_capacity_gb, number_of_disks)
 END SWITCH
END

ALGORITHM RAID_5_Capacity
INPUT: disk_capacity_gb, number_of_disks
OUTPUT: usable_capacity

BEGIN
 usable_capacity = disk_capacity_gb * (number_of_disks - 1)
 fault_tolerance = 1
 RETURN usable_capacity
END

ALGORITHM RAID_5_Write
INPUT: data_block, disk_array, stripe_size
OUTPUT: success_status

BEGIN
 stripe_index = data_block / stripe_size
 data_disks = number_of_disks - 1
 
 FOR i = 0 TO data_disks - 1 DO
 WRITE disk_array[i][stripe_index] = data_block[i]
 END FOR
 
 parity = Calculate_XOR(data_block[])
 WRITE disk_array[data_disks][stripe_index] = parity
 RETURN TRUE
END

ALGORITHM Calculate_XOR
INPUT: data_blocks[]
OUTPUT: parity_value

BEGIN
 parity_value = 0
 FOR each block IN data_blocks DO
 parity_value = XOR(parity_value, block)
 END FOR
 RETURN parity_value
END

ALGORITHM RAID_5_Recovery
INPUT: failed_disk_index, disk_array, number_of_disks, stripe_size
OUTPUT: recovered_data

BEGIN
 FOR each stripe IN disk_array DO
 reconstructed = 0
 FOR i = 0 TO number_of_disks - 1 DO
 IF i != failed_disk_index THEN
 reconstructed = XOR(reconstructed, disk_array[i][stripe])
 END IF
 END FOR
 recovered_data[stripe] = reconstructed
 END FOR
 RETURN recovered_data
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates RAID level selection and RAID-5 specific operations including capacity calculation, striping with parity generation, XOR-based parity calculation, and data recovery from a failed disk using XOR reconstruction.

examples.title: Python: RAID Storage Simulator
examples.description: Complete Python implementation simulating RAID levels 0, 1, 5, 6, and 10 with capacity calculations, write operations, and fault tolerance demonstration
examples.explanation: This Python implementation simulates different RAID storage levels showing capacity calculations, write operations with striping/mirroring, and fault tolerance. It demonstrates the core concepts of RAID storage including RAID-0 striping, RAID-1 mirroring, RAID-5/6 parity, and RAID-10 combined approach.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for write operations where n is data size

examples.title: C: Disk I/O and Storage Access Simulator
examples.description: C implementation simulating disk I/O operations, storage attachment types (DAS/NAS/SAN), and demonstrating how different attachment methods handle read/write requests
examples.explanation: This C implementation simulates disk I/O operations and demonstrates the three main storage attachment types: DAS (Direct-Attached Storage), NAS (Network-Attached Storage), and SAN (Storage Area Network). It calculates throughput and latency considering interface types, SSD vs HDD, RAID levels, and network overhead. The simulation shows why DAS has lowest latency, NAS adds network overhead, and SAN provides high throughput with dedicated network access.
